questions.question: Define Human-Computer Interaction and explain its multidisciplinary nature.
keyPoints: HCI is the study of interaction between humans and computers
It combines computer science, cognitive psychology, design, and anthropology
Focuses on design, implementation, and evaluation of interactive systems

questions.question: Explain the four basic interaction styles with examples for each.
keyPoints: Command Language: typing commands (DOS terminal)
Menu Selection: choosing from options (restaurant menu style)
Form-Fill-in: entering data in fields (registration form)
Direct Manipulation: direct object interaction (drag-drop files)

questions.question: State and explain Fitts's Law and its significance in interface design.
keyPoints: Time to acquire target = a + b log2(Distance/Size + 1)
Smaller targets take longer to acquire
Farther targets take longer to acquire
Design implication: make important buttons large and place them near cursor

questions.question: List and briefly explain the six usability goals in HCI.
keyPoints: Effectiveness: how well users accomplish tasks
Efficiency: resources required to accomplish tasks
Safety: protecting users from dangerous situations
Utility: providing appropriate functionality
Learnability: ease of learning to use system
Memorability: ease of remembering how to use after time

questions.question: What are the three categories of interface design principles? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: General principles: consistency, feedback, visibility
Guidelines: platform conventions, cultural conventions
Rules: Nielsen's heuristics, Shneiderman's golden rules
Examples help reduce learning load and errors

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'Mental Model' in HCI and its importance in interaction design.
keyPoints: Mental model is user's internal representation of system
Users predict system behavior based on mental models
Design should align with users' existing mental models
Poor mental models lead to confusion and errors

questions.question: Describe the fundamental interaction cycle between human and computer.
keyPoints: User formulates goal and plan
User executes action on interface
System receives input and processes
System provides feedback to user
User evaluates results and continues or stops
This loop continues until task is complete

questions.question: Explain Ben Shneiderman's Eight Golden Rules of Interface Design with practical examples for each.
keyPoints: 1) Strive for consistency: consistent actions produce consistent results
2) Enable frequent users to use shortcuts: shortcuts for expert users
3) Offer informative feedback: acknowledge every action
4) Design dialogs to yield closure: clear beginning-middle-end
5) Offer error prevention and simple error recovery: confirm dangerous actions
6) Permit easy reversal of actions: undo functionality
7) Support internal locus of control: users feel in command
8) Reduce short-term memory load: don't overwhelm with information
Each rule with practical UI example

questions.question: Explain the cognitive aspects that influence Human-Computer Interaction. Discuss how understanding these aspects helps in designing better interfaces.
keyPoints: Perceptual processes: vision, color, attention, reading
Cognitive load: working memory limitations
Attention: selective attention, divided attention
Memory: short-term (working) and long-term memory
Mental models: user expectations and beliefs
Problem-solving and decision making
Design implications: reduce cognitive load, use recognition over recall, provide feedback, chunk information

questions.question: Discuss Direct Manipulation as an interaction paradigm. List its advantages, disadvantages, and examples of systems employing this style.
keyPoints: Definition: direct interaction with visual representations
Characteristics: continuous representation, physical actions, rapid feedback, reversible operations
Advantages: intuitive, easy to learn, satisfying, reduces errors
Disadvantages: may be slower for experts, screen space limitations, complex to implement
Examples: Windows desktop, drag-drop operations, CAD software, video games
Comparison with command language interface

questions.question: Explain the five Gestalt principles of perceptual organization and their relevance in interface design.
keyPoints: 1) Proximity: elements near each other are perceived as related
2) Similarity: similar elements are perceived as related
3) Continuity: elements arranged in lines/curves are perceived as continuous
4) Closure: incomplete shapes are perceived as complete
5) Figure-Ground: distinction between foreground and background
Application in UI: grouping related items, visual hierarchy, icon design, layout organization
